messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: TL;DR: Enable a priority bit in the message header which modifies the order of traffic throughout the mesh. Proposal: Naval messages are sent with four precedence levels: Routine (R) within 6 hours, Priority (P) within 3 hours, Immediate (O) within 30 minutes, and Flash (Z) within 10 minutes, each with their respective symbols to indicate urgency. Explanation: The four precedence levels for sending out a Naval message along with their symbols and timelines are as follows: Routine (R): This level is for standard day-to-day operations that do not require immediate attention. Messages are expected to be delivered within 6 hours. Priority (P): Used for important tasks that require timely coordination and should be actioned before routine messages. These messages are typically delivered within 3 hours. Immediate (O): This level indicates that the message requires rapid action or decision. Immediate messages are to be delivered within 30 minutes. Flash (Z): The highest level of precedence, reserved for messages of extreme urgency, such as those vital to national survival. Flash messages are to be delivered within 10 minutes. The symbols R, P, O, and Z are used to designate these precedence levels. The timeline for each level of precedence indicates the maximum time allowed for a message to be delivered after its release by the originating station.
messages.content_html: TL;DR: Enable a priority bit in the message header which modifies the order of traffic throughout the mesh. Proposal: Naval messages are sent with four precedence levels: Routine (R) within 6 hours, Priority (P) within 3 hours, Immediate (O) within 30 minutes, and Flash (Z) within 10 minutes, each with their respective symbols to indicate urgency. Explanation: The four precedence levels for sending out a Naval message along with their symbols and timelines are as follows: Routine (R): This level is for standard day-to-day operations that do not require immediate attention. Messages are expected to be delivered within 6 hours. Priority (P): Used for important tasks that require timely coordination and should be actioned before routine messages. These messages are typically delivered within 3 hours. Immediate (O): This level indicates that the message requires rapid action or decision. Immediate messages are to be delivered within 30 minutes. Flash (Z): The highest level of precedence, reserved for messages of extreme urgency, such as those vital to national survival. Flash messages are to be delivered within 10 minutes. The symbols R, P, O, and Z are used to designate these precedence levels. The timeline for each level of precedence indicates the maximum time allowed for a message to be delivered after its release by the originating station.

messages.author: Bullit
messages.text: And even if not. I think with such an option, everyone thinks his message is „urgent“. And in the End, everyone is using Urgent and there‘s no difference left. I dont see a benefit from such a change. There should be no „class-thinking“ that some people start thinking that they are first class and sends everything priority etc.
messages.content_html: And even if not. I think with such an option, everyone thinks his message is „urgent“. And in the End, everyone is using Urgent and there‘s no difference left. I dont see a benefit from such a change. There should be no „class-thinking“ that some people start thinking that they are first class and sends everything priority etc.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: Does MeshCore have any concept of queuing messages that can't be delivered (say due to network congestion, being out-of-range, etc)?
messages.content_html: Does MeshCore have any concept of queuing messages that can't be delivered (say due to network congestion, being out-of-range, etc)?

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: For regular use, I agree. For use in times of network stress / user distress prioritization of traffic is super important. Although if there's no message queuing, then the question is moot.
messages.content_html: For regular use, I agree. For use in times of network stress / user distress prioritization of traffic is super important. Although if there's no message queuing, then the question is moot.
messages.reply_html:
Bullit
And even if not. I think with such an option, everyone thinks his message is „urgent“. And in the End, everyone is using Urgent and there‘s no difference left. I dont see a benefit from such a change. There should be no „class-thinking“ that some people start thinking that they are first class and sends everything priority etc.

messages.author: Bullit
messages.text: Dont get me wrong. I use, better said will use, because the network in my region is not established, meshcore as backup-com too for scenarios like power-outage etc. But even in an event like power-outage, other events etc i‘m fully aware that there are possibilities that my message may not go to the people i want to because of many people may want to reach out to family members too. Prioritizing would only lead, in my opinion, that all people knowing that feature will immediatly switch to „urgent“ which will not make any difference then.
messages.content_html: Dont get me wrong. I use, better said will use, because the network in my region is not established, meshcore as backup-com too for scenarios like power-outage etc. But even in an event like power-outage, other events etc i‘m fully aware that there are possibilities that my message may not go to the people i want to because of many people may want to reach out to family members too. Prioritizing would only lead, in my opinion, that all people knowing that feature will immediatly switch to „urgent“ which will not make any difference then.

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: Hhhmm....Tragedy of the commons then? What if we imposed a "fee" for use of the higher priority tiers? Could be you only get 3 "urgent' packets a day?
messages.content_html: Hhhmm....Tragedy of the commons then? What if we imposed a "fee" for use of the higher priority tiers? Could be you only get 3 "urgent' packets a day?

messages.author: Bullit
messages.text: And afaik there is some sort of queuing. Repeaters will relay one by one. If Repeater-Memory is full, packets get lost
messages.content_html: And afaik there is some sort of queuing. Repeaters will relay one by one. If Repeater-Memory is full, packets get lost

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: OK, no worries then. I was thinking of a use case like Blackout Comms devices and their Chatters protocol, which has both queing and message priority.
messages.content_html: OK, no worries then. I was thinking of a use case like Blackout Comms devices and their Chatters protocol, which has both queing and message priority.

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: Hah! So I'm part of the Flathead Mesh Network group in northwest Montana, USA. We operate a meshtastic network in Montana USA. 80+ nodes and we're seeing some scaling issues, so our advance tech team (Alpha Group) is starting to look into MeshCore.

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: We started out that way too. Just a few forward-thinking HAMs that wanted encrypted messaging (illegal on amateur radio). It really took off when one of the companies that operate point-2-point wireless radio towers agreed to host our radios. (edited)
messages.content_html: We started out that way too. Just a few forward-thinking HAMs that wanted encrypted messaging (illegal on amateur radio). It really took off when one of the companies that operate point-2-point wireless radio towers agreed to host our radios. (edited)

messages.author: Bullit
messages.text: Not so easy here… i found not a single point i could place a repeater without the need to contact an owner. Even far up there is always someone who owns land up there. If its not a person like and me, its the city, village or something else you need to ask for permission. Or you just place it Rogue-Style
